title: Your First Five Projects
summary: Five builds that take you from a first cut to a piece of furniture, in order.
description:
If you have a saw, a drill, and no idea where to start, start here.

These five are ordered on purpose. Each one adds exactly one new skill, and each one produces something you will actually keep — nothing here is a practice piece you throw away. By the end you will have made a glue-up, cut a joint, squared a case, and finished a surface, which is most of woodworking.

Do them in order. The temptation is to jump to the bookcase because it looks like real furniture. Resist it: the bookcase is the fourth one for a reason, and it will go badly if you have not yet learned to cut square.

steps.reason: The whole project is four cuts and some careful layout. That is the point: you learn to measure, mark, and cut to a line with almost nothing at stake, and you get a thing that hangs on a wall at the end of the afternoon.

Your first glue-up, and your first finished surface. A serving board forgives a lot — but it teaches you that flat matters, and it teaches you sanding, which is the skill beginners skip and then regret on every project after.

Your first CASE — four sides that have to meet each other squarely. Cedar is cheap and forgiving, and this one lives outdoors where nobody inspects your joinery. That combination is exactly what a first box should be.

Now the same idea at a scale where square actually matters: a bookcase that is out of square will rock, and you will see it every day. This is where the measuring habits from the coat rack start paying off.

Finish on something small and precise. After the bookcase you can build big; this one asks you to build TIGHT — accurate slots, clean glue lines, a surface people touch. It is the first project that looks bought rather than made.
